SF · USA

A Trip Book

San Francisco

A weekend by the Bay

Tap Next to begin →

Golden Gate Bridge

The fog rolled back just enough to catch the towers in full sun — that International Orange practically glowing against the hills.

Finished in 1937 under chief engineer Joseph Strauss, the bridge was painted its signature orange specifically to stay visible in San Francisco's thick coastal fog.

Alcatraz Island

From the boat, the old cell house and lighthouse looked almost peaceful — hard to picture it as the country's most notorious federal prison.

The federal penitentiary operated from 1934 to 1963 and now draws over a million visitors a year as a National Park Service site.

Fisherman's Wharf

The big ship's-wheel sign made a perfect landmark to meet up at, right before we wandered off in search of clam chowder.

Once a working fishing harbor, the Wharf still keeps a small commercial crab fleet docked alongside its restaurants and shops.

Lombard Street

Watching cars creep down the switchbacks between the hydrangeas was more entertaining than we expected — everyone on the sidewalk was grinning.

The famous one-block, eight-turn stretch was engineered in 1922 to make an otherwise 27% grade manageable for cars.

Alamo Square

The postcard view — Victorian rooftops lined up in front of the skyscrapers, just like every photo we'd seen, except this one was ours.

This row of Victorian houses, nicknamed the "Painted Ladies," was built between 1892 and 1896 and survived the 1906 earthquake and fire.

A Little Souvenir

Picked this up on the last afternoon — a tiny cable car for the keyring, so the trip rides along after we're home.

San Francisco's cable cars have run since 1873 and are the last manually operated cable car system left in the world.

The End

Golden Gate Bridge · Alcatraz Island · Fisherman's Wharf · Lombard Street · Alamo Square

A weekend by the Bay, six pages long — thanks for flipping through.
